votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logical equivalence between the boolean result and the universal quantification over the list elements.
The Python code correctly implements the logical equivalence defined in the Coq specification using the all() function, which matches the universal quantification over list elements.
The Python code correctly implements the logical equivalence between the boolean result and the universal quantification over the list elements.

The Coq spec states that result = true iff all elements x in l satisfy x < t. The Python code returns True iff result equals the boolean value of whether all elements in l are less than t. For concrete inputs, when result is True, both require all elements < t; when result is False, both require at least one element >= t. The Python correctly captures this biconditional relationship.

The Coq spec states result=true iff all elements x in l satisfy x < t. The Python code returns whether result equals the boolean value of all(x < t for x in l). For concrete inputs: if all elements are below t, Coq says result=true and Python checks result==True; if some element is >= t, Coq says result=false and Python checks result==False. Both produce the same concrete judgments.

The Coq spec states result=true iff all elements x in l satisfy x<t. The Python code returns True iff result equals the boolean value of all(x < t for x in l). For concrete inputs: when all elements are below t, Coq says result=true and Python checks result==True; when some element is >= t, Coq says result=false and Python checks result==False. Both agree on concrete boolean judgments.
Both specify that result is true exactly when all elements of l are less than t; Python’s result == all(x < t for x in l) matches the Coq equivalence.
Both require result to be true exactly when every element of l is strictly less than t; Python’s all over x < t matches Coq’s forall x, In x l -> x < t, including the empty list case.
Both define result to be true exactly when every element of l is less than t; Python’s all(x < t for x in l) matches the Coq forall/In condition, including the empty list case.
